title: What's Next: Convolutions
concept: This lesson closes Phase 1 by connecting every classical CV concept — pixels, color, formats, filters, transforms, noise, and frequency — into one idea: a learned convolution is a Sobel-style sliding kernel whose numbers came from data instead of a person, and that's exactly what Convolutional Neural Networks are built from.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which job do classical CV techniques like blur and geometric transforms still handle well today?

options.text: Preprocessing and augmenting data, plus fast lightweight tasks
Recognizing thousands of complex object categories on their own
Nothing — deep learning fully replaced them

exercises.explain: Right — classical CV still earns its keep on the simple, fast, mechanical jobs. Now let's zoom all the way out.

cards.title: The bridge you've been building
cards.body: Every lesson since 'What is a Digital Image?' has been building toward this moment. Grids of numbers, color channels, file formats, sliding filters, geometric transforms, noise, and frequency — you now have every classical piece needed to understand what a neural network actually does to an image.

cards.title: Where you've been: the classical CV journey
cards.body: You started with pixels and color, numbers on a grid, moved through file formats, how images get stored, then filters, sliding kernels for blur, sharpen, and edges, then geometric transforms, rotating, scaling, warping, then noise, what corrupts a signal, and finally frequency, seeing an image as patterns of change rather than individual pixels. Every one of those ideas reappears inside a neural network.

cards.title: Classical CV was the ladder
cards.body: Think of this entire phase as rungs on a ladder. You couldn't understand a learned convolution kernel without first understanding what a hand-designed kernel like Sobel even is, and you couldn't appreciate why a network handles noise well without first learning what noise and frequency mean in an image.

cards.title: Same sliding window, new source for the numbers
cards.body: A classical Sobel filter slides a fixed 3x3 grid of hand-picked numbers across an image to find edges. A Convolutional Neural Network's first layer slides a small grid of learned numbers across that same image — the same sliding-window math, except backpropagation chose the numbers instead of a mathematician.

cards.title: CNNs aren't a totally new idea
cards.body: It's tempting to think Convolutional Neural Networks appeared out of nowhere. They didn't — a convolution is literally the same sliding-kernel operation you've been using since blurring and Sobel, just with the filter values learned from data instead of set by hand.

cards.title: Phase 1 complete, Phase 2 begins
cards.body: Closing one door, opening the next:

left.text:
Pixels → color → formats →
filters → transforms → noise → frequency

right.text:
Learned kernels
doing that same sliding-window job

exercises.prompt: What is a Convolutional Neural Network's core operation most similar to, from everything you've learned in classical CV?

options.text: A Sobel-style filter sliding a small kernel of numbers across an image, except the numbers are learned instead of hand-designed
A file format converter that changes JPEG into PNG
A color space conversion, like RGB into grayscale
A geometric transform like rotating an image 90 degrees

exercises.explain: The sliding-kernel idea from blur and Sobel is exactly what a convolution is — just with learned numbers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The 'convolution' in Convolutional Neural Networks is essentially unrelated to the sliding-kernel filters, like blur and Sobel, you've already learned.
exercises.explain: False. A convolution is the same sliding-kernel math you've used all along, just with learned filter numbers.

exercises.sentence: Classical CV's biggest limitation was that a human had to hand-design a new filter for every object or condition; deep learning's fix is to let the network ___ its own filters from data.
exercises.explain: Learn is correct — that's the entire shift this phase has been building toward.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures the shift from classical CV to deep learning CV that this whole phase built up to?

options.text: The sliding-window filter idea stays the same — what changes is who chooses the numbers: a human engineer, or backpropagation learning from data
Deep learning throws away the sliding-window idea completely and starts from scratch
Classical CV and deep learning CV have nothing mathematically in common

exercises.explain: That's the throughline of the entire phase — same sliding-kernel core, different source for the filter numbers.

exercises.prompt: Looking back across this entire phase — pixels, color, file formats, filters, geometric transforms, noise, and frequency — what's the single biggest idea that carries forward into Convolutional Neural Networks?

options.text: An image is fundamentally a grid of numbers, and a small sliding kernel of numbers can extract meaningful patterns from it — classically hand-designed, or, coming up next, learned automatically by a network
None of the classical concepts matter anymore once you start using neural networks
Only file formats matter, since networks just need to load the file correctly
Color depth is the only classical concept with any relevance to deep learning

exercises.explain: That's the big idea! Every classical concept — pixels as numbers, filters as sliding kernels, transforms, noise, frequency — set you up to understand what's coming. Phase 1: Image & Signal Fundamentals is complete. Next up: Convolutions Deep Dive, where you'll see exactly how a network learns those kernel numbers for itself.
